title: Reasoning models are clearest in the boring parts
summary: A quieter essay on reasoning models, focused less on spectacle and more on what shows up in ordinary use.
topic: reasoning models as the tools got more useful and less theatrical
body: I wrote this down in March 2025 after thinking about reasoning models from the vantage point of a whiteboard full of edge cases rather than aspirations. The headline version of the topic interested me less than the sturdier question underneath it: what kind of world is this idea imagining into existence? With technology, that is usually the first thing to disappear. Most discussions rush from capability to performance to scale and leave the question of livability trailing behind. Reasoning models seemed to me like a good way to keep the older question in view.

The mechanism I cared about was mostly off to the side of the official explanation. Public language tends to grab the most visible element first: the model, the dashboard, the robot, the bit that photographs well. But most technologies become socially important through a second-order effect. They change pacing. They change the social split between people who get to stay complex and people who must become easy for the machine to parse. They change what can be ignored because some hidden workflow now catches it. Once you notice that shift, reasoning models stop looking like a gadget and start looking like a theory of everyday life with a power source attached.

There is also the old problem of atmosphere. Some technologies make people feel more room around themselves. Others make every surface feel like an input. Because it resists tidy measurement, that difference tends to vanish from the official accounting. But mood is one of the outputs. A system that steadily constricts attention or multiplies little permissions is telling you something honest about itself. It is part of what the product is. I have never found it convincing when people treat human texture as a sentimental distraction from performance.

Looking back from March 2025, I could already feel my own position shifting. I kept thinking about an older post of mine, “What evaluation taught me about building futures,” from February 2025. In that earlier note I was still describing the surface appeal. This time I cared less about the applause and more about what followed it: maintenance, politics, social sorting, and the emotional contract between people and the systems arranging their choices. I do not think that earlier optimism was wrong, exactly. Nothing about it was dishonest, it was simply the kind of optimism that arrives before enough lived contact.

I do not think the goal is to become anti-technology out of self-defense. The better goal is to become harder to flatter. The right response to a useful tool is not reverence but attention. If that sounds like a modest ambition, good. Most of the sanest writing I know begins modestly. The healthy move is to keep the claim narrow until something real forces it to widen. That is a lesson I hear again when I reread “A note on gig labor algorithms when the infrastructure finally became visible.”

A final note to myself from March 2025: the useful test is not whether a technology makes me feel early, but whether it makes the surrounding world more durable, more legible, and a little more generous. At this point the future matters to me mostly as a way of reading the demands a tool places on the people closest to it. If that sounds less glamorous than prediction, good. The glamorous version is usually how we miss the part that matters.

references.title: Evaluation gets harder right when it starts to matter
A note on gig labor algorithms when the infrastructure finally became visible
